mūla.devanāgarī: अद्वेष्टा सर्व-भूतानां मैत्रः करुण एव च | निर्ममो निरहंकारः सम-दुःख-सुखः क्षमी
mūla.iast: adveṣṭā sarva-bhūtānāṃ maitraḥ karuṇa eva ca | nirmamo nirahaṃkāraḥ sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ kṣamī
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: , आत्मनः दुःखहेतुमपि न किञ्चित् द्वेष्टि, सर्वाणि भूतानि आत्मत्वेन हि पश्यति
सर्वभूतानां किंतु मैत्रः मैत्री स्निग्धता तद्वान्
मित्रभावः मैत्री मित्रतया वर्तते इति मैत्रः
, करुणश्च उत्तमेषु द्वेषशून्यः, समेषु मित्रतया वर्तत इति मैत्रः
वृत्तस्वाध्यायादिकृताहंकारान्निष्क्रान्तः द्वेषरागयोरप्रवर्तकत्वेन समे दुःखसुखे यस्य सः

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: The bhakta who is dear to the Lord bears no hatred (dvesa) toward any being because he perceives all beings as the Self (atman) — to hate another is to hate oneself. Friendship (maitri) and compassion (karuna) arise naturally from this atma-darsana, not as moral duties imposed from without. Without the conceit of 'mine' (nirmama) and 'I' (nirahankara), pleasure and pain arrive equally and leave no residue; the knower simply abides unmoved, granting fearlessness to all — this is the mark of the samnyasi who sees brahman alone.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: The true bhakta understands that even those beings who harm or despise him do so under the Lord's impulsion proportionate to the bhakta's own past karma — recognizing this, he maintains maitri (loving regard) toward the very beings who wrong him, and karuna (compassion) toward their suffering. He is free of the notion 'this body is mine' (nirmama) and of the delusion that the body is the self (nirahankara), so pleasure and pain from contact cause neither elation nor agitation; unshaken equanimity (sama-duhkha-sukha) is the fruit of seeing all circumstances as Bhagavan's dispensation. Such a devotee, whose mind and intellect are offered entirely to Vasudeva, worships through karmayoga with no eye on results, and is therefore dear to the Lord.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: Madhvacarya did not comment on this sloka; the rendering below follows Dvaita philosophical logic. The jiva is eternally and absolutely distinct from Hari; all qualities catalogued here — non-hatred, friendliness, compassion, freedom from 'mine' and ego — are authentic expressions of the jiva's constitutional dependence (paratantrya) on Hari, not achievements of an expanding self. Equanimity in pleasure and pain and the power of forbearance (ksami) arise when the jiva rests in its proper subordinate station, knowing Hari alone is karta and bhokta.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Vallabhacarya opens: Bhagavan now declares in six slokas (beginning with 'advesta') the thirty-two marks of the pushti-bhakta who is the object of His love, because the Lord's grace alone — uncaused (nirhetuka) — is the root-cause of the bhakta's approach. Non-hatred, friendliness, and compassion are not virtues the bhakta acquires; they are the spontaneous fragrance of Krsna's prasada already operative in him. The bhakta does not work to be nirmama or nirahankara — Krsna's own anugrahika grace has already dissolved those illusions, making the devotee a fitting vessel for the Lord's lila.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Sridhara Svami reads these eight slokas (beginning 'advesta') as the qualities that swiftly (ksipram) produce the Lord's grace (paramesvara-prasada) for the devotee already described. Advesta toward all beings is glossed in three relational registers: toward superiors one is free of envy; toward equals one moves with friendly fellow-feeling (maitri); toward those in distress one extends compassion (karuna). Nirmama and nirahankara together remove the two great obstacles — possessiveness and body-identification — and from their removal equanimity in pain and pleasure (sama-duhkha-sukha) follows naturally; ksama (forbearance) is the stabilizing virtue that holds the whole disposition steady under provocation.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusudana Sarasvati contextualizes: the Lord, wishing to bring even those of middling qualification (manda-adhikari) to the aksar-upasana that is the highest goal, now praises in seven slokas the aksara-upasakas whose character Arjuna should emulate. The advesta who sees all beings as atman has no basis for hatred even when harmed; maitri is the natural warmth (snigdhata) arising from that vision; karuna is its active compassion making the devotee a source of fearlessness for all — he is, in essence, a paramahamsa-parivrajaka. Nirmama and nirahankara work together to extinguish the twin fires of attachment and pride, leaving a mind poised equally in pleasure and pain and a will incapable of retaliating under insult or blow — this steadiness is ksama in its most radical form.

तात्पर्य यह है कि सिद्ध भक्तका सुखियों और पुण्यात्माओंके प्रति
परन्तु भगवान्ने इन चारों हेतुओँको दोमें विभक्त कर दिया है --

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

post_generation_repairs.root_cause: ITRANS \, escape coerced to ASCII ? in gitasupersite HTML pipeline

so_what_questions: If advesta (non-hatred) rests on seeing all beings as atman or as Bhagavan's instruments, is it a moral achievement or a perceptual shift — and which comes first?

Schools split on whether these qualities are descriptive (the bhakta naturally has them as fruit of practice) or prescriptive (the aspirant must cultivate them as means). Does the text itself decide this question?
Nirmama (no 'mine') and nirahankara (no 'I') appear as a pair: is their order significant — must possessiveness dissolve before ego, or simultaneously?
Sama-duhkha-sukha (equanimity in pain and pleasure) is praised here; yet Krsna elsewhere weeps through Arjuna grief. How do the schools reconcile felt compassion with affective equanimity?

Ksama (forbearance) closes the verse. Sridhara calls it the stabilizing virtue. Sankara says the knower 'simply abides unmoved' when struck. Is forbearance an active discipline or the automatic consequence of right vision?
Vallabha insists these qualities appear in the bhakta only through Krsna's uncaused grace. Does this make human effort to cultivate them misguided, supplementary, or simply preparatory?
This is the first verse of the 12.13-19 catalog. Does the sequence matter — does non-hatred logically precede friendliness precede compassion, or is the list accumulative without hierarchy?

everyday_applications.advaita: When a colleague's criticism stings, pause and ask: 'Who is the one being criticized?' Sankara's advesta practice is not managing the emotion but questioning the identity that takes offence — the sting dissolves when the one who feels insulted is seen as a projected image, not bedrock reality.
everyday_applications.visistadvaita: In a difficult relationship, Ramanuja's practice is to trace the harm back: 'This person is acting under the Lord's guidance in proportion to something in my own past.' The shift is from 'why are they doing this to me' to 'what is Bhagavan teaching me through them' — which opens maitri where grievance had closed it.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's frame for nirmama in daily life: list the things you call 'mine' — money, reputation, relationships — and rehearse daily that you hold them in trust from Hari, who lent them and may recall them. The practice is not detachment as coldness but grateful stewardship, which is far more stable under loss than ownership ever was.
everyday_applications.suddhadvita: Vallabha's application is counter-intuitive: do not strain to become compassionate or ego-free. Instead, offer your attention to Krsna's lila — the stories, the name, the image — and watch the ego's grip loosen without direct assault. Pustimargi sadhana is the indirect path: grace does the inner work when the outer channel (sravana, kirtana) is held open.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's three-register reading of advesta becomes a practical social map: in professional hierarchies, check for envy of superiors; with peers, check whether you're extending genuine maitri or transactional courtesy; with those who are struggling, notice whether karuna is present or whether you're maintaining polite distance. The verse is a diagnostic across all three relational zones.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's synthesis offers the most integrative everyday practice: choose one person toward whom you feel chronic low-grade irritation and deliberately practice the paramahamsa quality he describes — grant them fearlessness by removing from your presence any judgment they might fear. This is ksama not as gritted-teeth endurance but as the active gift of safety, which transforms both the giver and the receiver.
